text:
September 3rd: Part 4 (93④, Kugatsu Mikka (Yon)) is the 88 th chapter of the Hunter × Hunter manga, written and illustrated by Yoshihiro Togashi.

Synopsis

At the Preview Market, a man claims that while the wooden vault itself may be real, the jewelry inside might not be. However, another person speaks up and says that they confirmed with their magnifying glasses that they are genuine antiques. The first man says that he hasn't checked them all and that even if they are all real, it doesn't necessarily mean that they are the same age as the vault itself. Killua asks Zepile what the man is doing, and he replies that he's sowing doubt to lower the price. Killua wonders if they should stop them, but Zepile says that it's common at previews since everyone wants the best deal.

Zepile tells Gon and Killua to watch people closely and ask if they need any help. Gon approaches and stares up at the man who initially spoke. The man holds a ring in his hand and asks if it belongs to him, assuring Gon that he won't switch them since each item has a gallery tag. Zepile says that the tags wouldn't stop anyone from doing so, and the man asks him who he is. Zepile tells him that the kids are his clients and that he can confirm that both the vault and jewelry are genuine.

One of the men asks if they have a certificate of authenticity, and Zepile replies that they don't, with the two men saying that it's a waste of time if they're supposed to take him at his word. The other appraisers ask him why he didn't get one, and Zepile says that they didn't have enough time before the auction and that his clients need the money before the end of the year. As he tells the appraisers that they will withdraw the item if it doesn't reach the price they want, Gon wonders why Zepile said by the end of the year and Killua tells him that the appraisers would try to scam them if they thought that they needed the money right then.

Zepile then assures them that they can get an independent appraisal after the auction and that they will give the buyer a full refund if the item turns out to be fake. The appraisers seem convinced by the offer, but the initial objector again speaks up and says that wooden vaults are tricky to appraise because of the numerous doctoring methods. He explains that the contents in the vault could have been replaced, and then lists out five kinds of transplants to show his knowledge of the topic. He adds that it will be difficult to authenticate the vault as well since while the wood itself may be over 300 years old, it doesn't mean it was carved 300 years ago (called a facelift).

As the other appraisers mutter amongst themselves, Zepile pushes back and says that it isn't a facelift since the glue on the seam is old and oxidized, with no trace of a second layer. The man counters by saying that the glue that was previously used melts under heat, and that it's used during appraisals as well as in scams. Zepile realizes that the man is well informed, and he continues by explaining that a forger would melt the glue and then reuse it, with the seal appearing still intact (called cauterization). The other appraisers again mutter amongst themselves and begin to further doubt the authenticity of the item. Zepile tries to assure them that cauterization would leave scorch marks and that the glue would have slight discoloration, but the man replies that said discoloration can only be seen by the naked eye and that even professionals fail to see it sometimes. The appraisers talk amongst themselves even more, and Zepile realizes that they are starting to hesitate.

After some time passes and the three leave the market, Zepile blows a puff of smoke from his cigarette, upset that they had to give a lecture on scams in front of everyone and guessing that they're the type to drive down prices as much as possible. He adds that they criticize items to cause controversy, even adding poorer items while they pretend to appraise the others, and Gon remembers that Zepile warned them about people pulling tricks. He asks if they will make a good profit, and Zepile replies that they won't know until the start of the auction since it depends on the item order. He adds that there were a few good appraisers in the group and that a bidding war for the item could start among them.

Gon then asks about other doctoring methods, eager to learn about them since they're like magic tricks. As Killua playfully slaps his face and reminds him that they're trying to make money, Zepile realizes why Gon is so intriguing to him, telling himself that the boy doesn't care whether something is good or bad. He remembers that Gon didn't show any sign of disapproval or greed when he mentioned making counterfeits or when the dealers were lecturing about the wooden vault. He says that Gon is just purely curious about things as long as they impress him, worried that he's walking a fine line and saying that there would be no point trying to appraise him.

Zepile agrees to teach them more about doctoring methods, buying a toy in a capsule and explaining that the container is the hardest to appraise when examining a wooden vault. He adds that the carving is usually imprecise because it was designed to hide the riches inside, which is why one can't use quality to authenticate it. After opening the capsule and closing it again, Killua points out that the seam is the key. Zepile confirms this and says that it was cleverly hidden with wood shavings and paint. Killua says that if the oxidation looks the right age, it's the real thing, but Gon brings up the concept of cauterization.

Zepile says that Gon is right and that the vault's contents could have been replaced and then the seam glued again since even professionals struggle to identify heat discoloration. He adds that if scammers use this technique too often, people begin to avoid sealed vaults and the market dries up. However, he explains that the scammers then showcase a real vault opened and replace the contents with cheap jewelry (called an autopsy). He says that even expert appraisers can be fooled by the scam since it's assumed that one wouldn't have it opened if it were fake. He then says that when that scam becomes overused, another one is employed that fools even the most observant appraisers. Killua asks if the vaults were sealed, and Zepile says that they were never opened and the seams weren't cauterized. They guess that the jewelry was fake, but Zepile says that it's the wrong answer.

Killua then realizes that they must have taken the jewelry out through another hole, and Zepile goes through the process of ostomy in detail, explaining that they would create another hole away from the seam, replace the real jewelry with fake ones, and then patch up the hole. He says that while the process may be simple, it can fool even the most veteran of appraisers since they assume that the contents have to be real if the glue isn't discolored. Gon lets out a big sigh and says that he's impressed, and Zepile explains that it's why appraisers are so suspicious when items look too perfect. Gon's Beatle-07 phone suddenly rings with a call from Leorio, who reveals that an informant is tailing members of the Phantom Troupe and sending him pictures with his cell phone camera. He receives an image of Machi and Nobunaga with a message from the informant asking him to respond. Gon and Killua quickly make their way through the marketplace to meet up with Leorio.
